label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's go through the passage carefully to find out which statement is true. First, let's look at each option: (A) The lottery system is certainly a good way to allocate school places. - The passage mentions that the government is reviewing the use of lotteries because many children aren't getting into their first-choice schools. It also says that lotteries were introduced in some areas to prevent middle-class parents from gaming the system. However, the passage doesn't definitively say that the lottery system is "certainly a good way." In fact, it shows mixed results: in Brighton and Hove, there was a slight improvement in getting first-choice schools, but more than 5% ended up in a school not on their choices. So, the passage doesn't state that the lottery system is certainly good, just that it's being used and reviewed. (B) Over one in five children fail to get into their first-choice schools in parts of Britain. - The first sentence of the passage says that "more than 20 percent of children are failing to get into their first-choice schools in parts of the country." This directly supports statement B as being true. (C) The lottery system is welcomed by parents around twenty-five local authorities. - The passage states that lotteries are used in around twenty-five local authorities at the government's suggestion. However, it doesn't mention whether parents in these areas welcome the system. There's no information about the parents' reception or opinion, so we can't say this statement is true based on the passage. (D) The number of applications for secondary school places is falling this year. - The passage actually says the opposite: "As the recession forces more parents to consider a state education rather than a private one for their children, more than a third of local authorities have noted rises in the number of applications for secondary school places." So statement D is false. Therefore,
